questions.question: Let p, q, r denote the statements "It is raining", "It is cold", and "It is pleasant", respectively. Then the statement "It is not raining and it is pleasant, and it is not pleasant only if it is raining and it is cold" is represented by  A. (not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q))   B. (not p and r) and ((p and q) -> not r)   C. (not p and r) or ((p and q) -> not r)   D. (not p and r) or (r -> (p and q))
questions.answer: A. (not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q))
questions.to_find: The propositional-logic formula for the given English sentence.

understand.plain: Translate the English clause by clause. 'It is not raining and it is pleasant' is not p and r. The tricky part is 'A only if B', which in logic means A -> B (B is necessary for A). Here 'it is not pleasant only if it is raining and it is cold' becomes (not r) -> (p and q). Joining the two parts with 'and' gives (not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q)), which is option A.

keywords.explain: 'A only if B' means A -> B: B is a necessary condition for A, so A cannot hold without B
keywords.example: 'you pass only if you study' = pass -> study

keywords.explain: 'if B then A' makes B sufficient (B -> A); 'A only if B' makes B necessary (A -> B)
keywords.example: rain is sufficient for wet; oxygen is necessary for fire

keywords.explain: map each English connective to its symbol: 'and' = and, 'not' = not, 'only if' = ->
keywords.example: 'not raining and pleasant' = not p and r

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Translating the English sentence to logic'><title>English to propositional logic</title><desc>The sentence splits into not p and r, joined by AND to the only-if clause not r implies p and q.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='25' y='50' width='210' height='90' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>part 1</text><text x='130' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>not p and r</text><text x='130' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>not raining, pleasant</text><text x='270' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>and</text><rect x='320' y='50' width='220' height='90' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='430' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>part 2 (only if)</text><text x='430' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>not r -> (p and q)</text><text x='430' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>not pleasant only if rain and cold</text><text x='280' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q))</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The sentence splits into 'not p and r' joined by AND to the only-if clause 'not r -> (p and q)', giving option A.

given.aim: Build the correct propositional formula for the compound English statement.

terms.meaning: p = it is raining, q = it is cold, r = it is pleasant
terms.connects: the atoms from which the formula is assembled

terms.meaning: the first conjunct: it is not raining and it is pleasant
terms.example: not p and r is True when p is False and r is True
terms.connects: joined by the outer 'and' to the second part

terms.meaning: the 'only if' clause: it is not pleasant only if it is raining and it is cold
terms.example: if not r holds then p and q must hold
terms.connects: second conjunct of the whole statement

given.plan: Translate 'X only if Y' as X -> Y, render each English fragment, and conjoin the two parts with 'and'.

steps.title: Translate the first part
steps.formula_raw: 'it is not raining and it is pleasant' = not p and r
steps.apply: 'It is not raining' is not p and 'it is pleasant' is r; joining with 'and' gives not p and r.
steps.note: A straightforward word-for-word mapping of and / not.

steps.title: Translate the 'only if' clause
steps.formula_raw: X only if Y  ==  X -> Y
steps.apply: 'it is not pleasant only if it is raining and it is cold': the subject 'it is not pleasant' = not r, the condition 'it is raining and it is cold' = p and q. 'X only if Y' is X -> Y, so this is not r -> (p and q).
steps.note: Trap: 'only if' is implication forward (not r -> ...), not the reverse.

steps.title: Conjoin the two parts
steps.formula_raw: (not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q))
steps.apply: The overall sentence connects part 1 and part 2 with 'and', giving (not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q)). This matches option A exactly.
steps.note: B uses the wrong implication direction; C and D use 'or' instead of 'and'.

solution.result: The statement is (not p and r) and (not r -> (p and q)), so the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is 'X only if Y' = X -> Y; getting that direction right (and using 'and' to join the parts) singles out option A.

questions.question: Let p and q be two propositions. Consider the following two formulae in propositional logic.  S_1 : (not p and (p or q)) -> q   S_2 : q -> (not p and (p or q))  Which one of the following choices is correct?  A. Both S_1 and S_2 are tautologies.   B. S_1 is a tautology but S_2 is not a tautology   C. S_1 is not a tautology but S_2 is a tautology   D. Neither S_1 nor S_2 is a tautology
questions.answer: B. S_1 is a tautology but S_2 is not a tautology
questions.to_find: Which of S_1 and S_2 are tautologies.

understand.plain: First simplify the shared subformula: not p and (p or q) = (not p and p) or (not p and q) = False or (not p and q) = not p and q. So S_1 is (not p and q) -> q, whose antecedent contains q, hence it is always True: a tautology. S_2 is q -> (not p and q); take q True and p True: antecedent True but not p and q = False, so S_2 fails - not a tautology. Hence B.

keywords.explain: not p and (p or q) distributes to (not p and p) or (not p and q); the first term is False, leaving not p and q
keywords.example: not p and (p or q) == not p and q

keywords.explain: a formula True under every assignment of its variables
keywords.example: (a and q) -> q is always True

keywords.explain: one row making an implication's antecedent True and consequent False breaks the tautology
keywords.example: for S_2 take p = True, q = True

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='S1 tautology, S2 not'><title>S1 versus S2</title><desc>S1 simplifies to (not p and q) -> q which is always True; S2 fails when p and q are both True.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='50' width='200' height='130' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>S1</text><text x='140' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(not p and q) -> q</text><text x='140' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>tautology</text><text x='140' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>antecedent has q</text><rect x='320' y='50' width='200' height='130' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='420' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>S2</text><text x='420' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q -> (not p and q)</text><text x='420' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>not taut</text><text x='420' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>fails at p=T,q=T</text><text x='280' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S1 always True, S2 has a counterexample => B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: S1 simplifies to (not p and q) -> q, always True, while S2 = q -> (not p and q) fails when p and q are both True; so S1 is a tautology and S2 is not.

given.aim: Decide the tautology status of S_1 and S_2 and pick the matching option.

terms.meaning: the shared subformula that simplifies to not p and q
terms.example: with p False, q True it is True; with p True it is False
terms.connects: appears as antecedent of S_1 and consequent of S_2

terms.term: S_1 = (not p and (p or q)) -> q
terms.meaning: implication whose antecedent reduces to not p and q
terms.example: antecedent True forces q True, so S_1 True

terms.term: S_2 = q -> (not p and (p or q))
terms.meaning: implication whose consequent reduces to not p and q
terms.example: q True with p True makes consequent False
terms.connects: shown not to be a tautology

given.plan: Simplify not p and (p or q) to not p and q via distribution, then test each implication for a falsifying row.

steps.title: Simplify the shared subformula
steps.formula_raw: not p and (p or q) == (not p and p) or (not p and q) == not p and q
steps.apply: Distribute not p over (p or q): (not p and p) or (not p and q). Since not p and p = False, the expression reduces to not p and q.
steps.note: The contradictory term not p and p drops out.

steps.title: S_1 is a tautology
steps.apply: S_1 becomes (not p and q) -> q. If the antecedent not p and q is True then q is True, so the consequent holds; the implication can never fail. S_1 is a tautology.
steps.note: q appears inside the antecedent, so it is automatically delivered.

steps.title: S_2 is not a tautology
steps.apply: S_2 becomes q -> (not p and q). Take p = True, q = True: antecedent q = True, but not p and q = False and True = False, so S_2 = True -> False = False. One counterexample defeats it.
steps.note: The consequent demands not p, which q alone cannot guarantee.

solution.result: S_1 is a tautology but S_2 is not, so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Simplifying not p and (p or q) to not p and q is the key: it makes S_1 a (tautological) implication with q in the antecedent, while S_2 still needs not p, which q cannot force, so S_2 fails.

questions.question: Choose the correct choice(s) regarding the following propositional logic assertion S:  S : ((P and Q) -> R) -> ((P and Q) -> (Q -> R))  A. S is neither a tautology nor a contradiction   B. S is a tautology   C. S is a contradiction   D. The antecedent of S is logically equivalent to the consequent of S
questions.to_find: Which statements about S are correct (tautology / contradiction status and antecedent-consequent equivalence).

understand.plain: Write A for P and Q, so S is (A -> R) -> (A -> (Q -> R)). Notice that A = P and Q already forces Q True, so under A the inner Q -> R behaves exactly like R. Therefore the consequent A -> (Q -> R) is logically the same as A -> R, the antecedent. An implication of the form X -> X is always True, so S is a tautology (B), and the antecedent equals the consequent (D).

keywords.explain: a formula True under every assignment; here S is True for all P, Q, R
two formulas with the same truth table; the antecedent and consequent of S match exactly
keywords.example: (P and Q) -> R == (P and Q) -> (Q -> R)

keywords.explain: inside the scope where P and Q is True, Q is True, so Q -> R collapses to R
keywords.example: given Q True, Q -> R == R

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='S is X implies X, a tautology'><title>Antecedent equals consequent</title><desc>Since P and Q forces Q, the consequent (P and Q)->(Q->R) equals the antecedent (P and Q)->R, so S is X -> X, a tautology.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='60' width='210' height='90' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='135' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>antecedent</text><text x='135' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(P and Q) -> R</text><text x='280' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>==</text><rect x='320' y='60' width='210' height='90' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='425' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>consequent</text><text x='425' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(P and Q) -></text><text x='425' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(Q -> R)</text><text x='280' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S = X -> X  =>  tautology (B), and X == X (D)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Because P and Q forces Q, the consequent (P and Q)->(Q->R) equals the antecedent (P and Q)->R, so S has the form X -> X, a tautology, and antecedent equals consequent.

given.aim: Classify S and check whether its antecedent and consequent are equivalent.

terms.meaning: shorthand for the common antecedent; note A True implies Q True
terms.example: if A holds then both P and Q are True
terms.connects: lets us treat S as (A -> R) -> (A -> (Q -> R))

terms.meaning: the left side of the outer implication
terms.example: False only when A True and R False

terms.meaning: the right side; under A (so Q True) it reduces to A -> R
terms.example: with Q True, Q -> R = R, so this is A -> R
terms.connects: turns out equal to the antecedent

given.plan: Use that A = P and Q forces Q True to simplify Q -> R to R inside the consequent, showing antecedent == consequent and S = X -> X.

steps.title: Show antecedent and consequent are equivalent
steps.formula_raw: Given (P and Q) True, Q is True, so Q -> R == R; hence (P and Q) -> (Q -> R) == (P and Q) -> R
steps.apply: Let A = P and Q. When A is True, Q is True, so Q -> R has the same value as R. When A is False, both A -> R and A -> (Q -> R) are vacuously True. So the consequent A -> (Q -> R) is logically equivalent to the antecedent A -> R. This proves D.
steps.note: Inside the assumption A = P and Q, the literal Q is already guaranteed.

steps.title: Conclude S is a tautology
steps.apply: With X = (P and Q) -> R, Step 1 shows S = X -> X. Any formula implies itself, so S is True under every assignment: S is a tautology (B), hence it is not a contradiction and is not 'neither' (A and C are false).
steps.note: X -> X never has a True antecedent with a False consequent.

solution.result: S is a tautology and its antecedent equals its consequent, so the correct choices are B and D.

questions.formula_note: Because P and Q forces Q True, Q -> R collapses to R, making S an X -> X self-implication: hence S is a tautology (B) and antecedent == consequent (D).

questions.question: Let p and q be the following propositions:  p : Fail grade can be given.   q : Student scores more than 50% marks.  Consider the statement: "Fail grade cannot be given when student scores more than 50% marks."  Which one of the following is the CORRECT representation of the above statement in propositional logic?  A. q -> not p   B. q -> p   C. p -> q   D. not p -> q
questions.to_find: The propositional formula for the given English statement.

understand.plain: Identify the trigger and the result. The phrase 'when student scores more than 50% marks' is the condition (q), and 'fail grade cannot be given' is the result (not p). 'Result when condition' means condition -> result, i.e. q -> not p. So whenever q is True (student scored above 50%), p (fail can be given) must be False.

keywords.explain: 'Y when X' translates to X -> Y; the 'when' clause is the antecedent (the condition)
keywords.example: 'I stay in when it rains' = rain -> stay in

keywords.explain: 'fail grade cannot be given' negates p, giving not p
the condition introduced by 'when' goes on the left of the arrow, the consequence on the right
keywords.example: 'not p when q' = q -> not p

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Translating the fail-grade statement'><title>q -> not p</title><desc>The when-clause q (scores above 50%) is the antecedent; the result not p (fail cannot be given) is the consequent.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='60' width='190' height='90' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='135' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>condition (when)</text><text x='135' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q</text><text x='135' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>scores > 50%</text><text x='270' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>-></text><rect x='320' y='60' width='200' height='90' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='420' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>result</text><text x='420' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>not p</text><text x='420' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>fail cannot be given</text><text x='280' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>q -> not p</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 'when' clause q (scores above 50%) is the antecedent and the result not p (fail cannot be given) is the consequent, giving q -> not p.

given.aim: Encode the English sentence as the right implication over p and q.

terms.example: 'fail grade cannot be given' = not p

terms.connects: becomes the antecedent of the implication

terms.meaning: if the student scores above 50%, then fail cannot be given
terms.example: q True forces not p True, i.e. p False
terms.connects: the formula that matches the sentence

given.plan: Read 'result when condition' as condition -> result: put q (the when-clause) as antecedent and not p (cannot be given) as consequent.

steps.title: Negate the result clause
steps.formula_raw: 'fail grade cannot be given' = not p
steps.apply: Since p means 'fail grade can be given', the clause 'fail grade cannot be given' is its negation, not p.

steps.title: Place the 'when' clause as antecedent
steps.apply: 'X result when Y condition' means Y -> X. Here the condition is q (scores above 50%) and the result is not p, so the statement is q -> not p, which is option A.
steps.note: B, C, D place the wrong term as antecedent or drop the negation.

solution.result: The statement is q -> not p, so the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: The key is reading 'result when condition' as condition -> result; with the condition q and the negated result not p, the sentence is exactly q -> not p.

questions.question: Let x and y be two propositions. Which of the following statements is a tautology / are tautologies?  A. (not x and y) => (y => x)   B. (x and not y) => (not x => y)   C. (not x and y) => (not x => y)   D. (x and not y) => (y => x)
questions.to_find: Which of the four implications are tautologies.

understand.plain: Each option is an outer implication whose antecedent is a conjunction of two literals, so the antecedent is True for exactly one (x, y) assignment. An implication is a tautology iff on that single assignment the consequent is also True (it is vacuously True on all other rows). Check the consequent on the forcing row: B, C, D all give a True consequent there, so they are tautologies; A gives a False consequent, so A is not.

keywords.explain: an implication with a False antecedent is automatically True; only the row where the antecedent is True can break it
keywords.example: (x and not x) => anything is a tautology

keywords.explain: a conjunction of two literals pins both variables, so there is exactly one row where the antecedent is True
keywords.example: not x and y is True only at x=F, y=T

keywords.explain: X => Y is False only when X is True and Y is False
keywords.example: T => F = False; everything else = True

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Tautology check of four implications'><title>Which are tautologies</title><desc>B, C and D are tautologies; A is falsified at its single forcing row x false y true.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='25' y='45' width='120' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='85' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='85' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>x=F,y=T</text><rect x='155' y='45' width='120' height='150' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='215' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='215' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>taut</text><text x='215' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>x=T,y=F</text><rect x='285' y='45' width='120' height='150' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='345' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='345' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>taut</text><text x='345' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>x=F,y=T</text><rect x='415' y='45' width='120' height='150' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='475' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><text x='475' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>taut</text><text x='475' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>x=T,y=F</text><text x='280' y='228' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>B, C, D true on their forcing row; A false there</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: B, C and D are tautologies because their consequents are True on the single row that makes the antecedent True; A is falsified at x=False, y=True.

given.aim: Identify which of the four implications are True under every assignment of x and y.

terms.meaning: propositional variables, each True or False
terms.connects: a tautology must hold on all four rows

terms.meaning: each option's left side is True for exactly one assignment
terms.example: x and not y is True only at x=T, y=F
terms.connects: that single row is the only place the implication can fail

terms.meaning: the inner implication on the right side, evaluated on the forcing row
terms.example: for B the consequent not x => y at x=T,y=F is F => F = True
terms.connects: if True on the forcing row, the whole option is a tautology

given.plan: For each option find the unique row making the antecedent True, then evaluate the consequent there; True means tautology, False means not.

steps.title: Option A fails on its forcing row
steps.formula_raw: (not x and y) => (y => x);  forcing row x=F, y=T
steps.apply: not x and y is True only at x=False, y=True. There the consequent y => x = True => False = False, so A = True => False = False. A is not a tautology.
steps.note: y True with x False breaks y => x.

steps.title: Option B is a tautology
steps.formula_raw: (x and not y) => (not x => y);  forcing row x=T, y=F
steps.apply: x and not y is True only at x=True, y=False. There not x => y = False => False = True, so the consequent holds; on all other rows the antecedent is False (vacuously True). B is a tautology.
steps.note: A False antecedent (not x) makes the consequent True automatically.

steps.title: Option C is a tautology
steps.formula_raw: (not x and y) => (not x => y);  forcing row x=F, y=T
steps.apply: not x and y is True only at x=False, y=True. There not x => y = True => True = True, so the consequent holds; all other rows are vacuously True. C is a tautology.
steps.note: On the forcing row both not x and y are True, so the consequent is T => T.

steps.title: Option D is a tautology
steps.formula_raw: (x and not y) => (y => x);  forcing row x=T, y=F
steps.apply: x and not y is True only at x=True, y=False. There y => x = False => True = True, so the consequent holds; all other rows are vacuously True. D is a tautology.
steps.note: y False makes y => x vacuously True.

solution.result: B, C and D are tautologies; A is falsified at x=False, y=True, so the answer is B, C, D.

questions.formula_note: Each antecedent is a two-literal conjunction with a single forcing row; checking the consequent there (vacuous truth elsewhere) shows B, C, D hold but A fails at x=False, y=True.

formulas.name: Conjunction Implies Shared-Term Disjunction
formulas.plain: (a and b) -> (b or c) is a tautology
formulas.whenToUse: To recognise a tautology where a conjunct of the antecedent also appears in the consequent disjunction.

terms.means: shared proposition appearing in both sides

formulas.trap: It works because b is in both sides; (a or b) -> (b and c) is NOT a tautology.

formulas.name: 'Only If' as Implication
formulas.plain: 'A only if B' means A -> B (B is necessary for A)
formulas.whenToUse: When translating an English sentence containing 'only if' into propositional logic.

terms.means: the statement that depends on the condition
the necessary condition introduced by 'only if'

formulas.trap: 'A only if B' is A -> B, NOT B -> A; 'if B then A' (sufficient) is the reverse direction.

formulas.name: 'When' Clause as Implication
formulas.plain: 'Y when X' means X -> Y (the 'when' clause is the antecedent)
formulas.whenToUse: When translating a sentence of the form 'result when condition' into propositional logic.
formulas.trap: The 'when' clause is the antecedent (left of ->); do not swap it with the result.

formulas.name: Distributive Law (and over or)
formulas.plain: A and (B or C) == (A and B) or (A and C)
formulas.whenToUse: To expand a conjunction over a disjunction (or vice versa) when simplifying a formula.

terms.means: the factor distributed across the disjunction

formulas.trap: After distributing, watch for contradictory terms like not p and p that simplify to False.

formulas.name: Self-Implication Tautology
formulas.plain: X -> X is always True for any formula X
formulas.whenToUse: When a formula reduces to the shape X -> X, concluding immediately that it is a tautology.

terms.means: any propositional formula appearing on both sides of the implication

formulas.trap: Only applies once both sides are shown logically equivalent; superficially different sides may still be equivalent.

formulas.name: Antecedent-Context Simplification of an Implication
formulas.plain: If the antecedent A guarantees Q, then A -> (Q -> R) is equivalent to A -> R
formulas.whenToUse: When the antecedent of an implication already forces a literal that appears in the consequent.

terms.means: the governing antecedent (e.g. P and Q)
a literal forced True by A

formulas.trap: This holds only where A is True; where A is False both sides are vacuously True, so equivalence still holds overall.

formulas.name: Vacuous Truth (False Antecedent)
formulas.plain: An implication with a False antecedent is automatically True
formulas.whenToUse: To test an implication for being a tautology by only checking rows where the antecedent is True.

terms.means: the consequent, whose value is irrelevant when the antecedent is False

formulas.trap: Only the rows with a True antecedent can falsify an implication; a two-literal conjunction antecedent has exactly one such row.
